cards.question: Grouping similar things according to some basis or criteria is called classification. We can keep dissimilar objects away by the method of classification.
cards.text_html: Grouping similar things according to some basis or criteria is called classification. We can keep dissimilar objects away by the method of classification.

cards.question: A substance or a thing that is used to make different objects is called material.
cards.text_html: A substance or a thing that is used to make different objects is called material.

cards.question: We classify objects around us because it gives us the following advantages: Classification of objects into groups makes it easier to locate them. Classification makes it convenient to study the properties of objects of one category and also observe any pattern in these properties.
cards.text_html:
We classify objects around us because it gives us the following advantages:

  1. Classification of objects into groups makes it easier to locate them.
  2. Classification makes it convenient to study the properties of objects of one category and also observe any pattern in these properties.

cards.question: All objects around you have definite shapes. For example, eggs are oval and ice-cream cones are conical in shape.
cards.text_html: All objects around you have definite shapes. For example, eggs are oval and ice-cream cones are conical in shape.

cards.question: Materials A substance that is used in making different objects is called material. All objects are made of one more kind of material. Material examples: cotton, silk, iron, plastic, and glass.
cards.text_html:
Materials

  • A substance that is used in making different objects is called material.
  • All objects are made of one more kind of material.
  • Material examples: cotton, silk, iron, plastic, and glass.

cards.question: Natural material: Natural materials are materials that come from plants, animals, and rocks.
cards.text_html: Natural material: Natural materials are materials that come from plants, animals, and rocks.

cards.question: Source Natural materials Objects Plants Wood Chair, table, cupboard Rubber Balloon, tyre, glove Cotton T-shirt, cloth, scarf Animals Fur Hat, sweater Silk Cloth Leather Handbag, belt, shoes Rocks Metal Spoon, kettle Soil Vase, clay pot
cards.text_html:
Source Natural materials Objects
Plants Wood Chair, table, cupboard
  Rubber Balloon, tyre, glove
  Cotton T-shirt, cloth, scarf
Animals Fur Hat, sweater
  Silk Cloth
  Leather Handbag, belt, shoes
Rocks Metal Spoon, kettle
  Soil Vase, clay pot

cards.question: Man-made material: Man-made materials are materials obtained from natural materials through a chemical process.
cards.text_html: Man-made material: Man-made materials are materials obtained from natural materials through a chemical process.

cards.question: Man-Made materials Objects Plastic Container, ruler, bottle, straw, cup Nylon, rayon, polyester Net, tent, raincoat, umbrella
cards.text_html:
Man-Made materials Objects
Plastic Container, ruler, bottle, straw, cup
Nylon, rayon, polyester Net, tent, raincoat, umbrella

cards.question: An object made of different materials: Object Materials from which the given object can be made Chair Wood, Iron, Plastic, Concrete Clothes Cotton, Wood, Silk Coin Copper, Gold Pen Plastic, Steel
cards.text_html:
An object made of different materials:

Object Materials from which the given object can be made
Chair Wood, Iron, Plastic, Concrete
Clothes Cotton, Wood, Silk
Coin Copper, Gold
Pen Plastic, Steel

cards.question: Different types of objects made of same material Material Objects Wood Chair, Table, Cupboard Rubber Ballon, Tyre, Glove Cotton T-shirt, Cloth, Scarf Fur Hat, Sweater Silk Cloth Leather Handbag, Belt, Shoes Metal Spoon, Kettle Soil Vase, Claypot
cards.text_html:
Different types of objects made of same material

Material Objects
Wood Chair, Table, Cupboard
Rubber Ballon, Tyre, Glove
Cotton T-shirt, Cloth, Scarf
Fur Hat, Sweater
Silk Cloth
Leather Handbag, Belt, Shoes
Metal Spoon, Kettle
Soil Vase, Claypot

cards.question: An object made up of a combination of materials Object Combination of materials they made of Tea Water, tea leaves, sugar, milk Vegetable curry Oil, vegetables, chili, salt, water
cards.text_html:
An object made up of a combination of materials

Object Combination of materials they made of
Tea Water, tea leaves, sugar, milk
Vegetable curry Oil, vegetables, chili, salt, water

cards.question: Lustrous materials are those that have a shine on them. They are usually metals. For example, Gold, silver, and mist metals are lustrous in nature.
cards.text_html:
• Lustrous materials are those that have a shine on them.
  • They are usually metals.
  • For example, Gold, silver, and mist metals are lustrous in nature.

cards.question: Non-lustrous materials are dull in appearance. For example wood, plastics, cotton
cards.text_html:
Non-lustrous materials are dull in appearance.
For example wood, plastics, cotton

cards.question: Materials that can be compressed or scratched easily are called soft materials For example: cotton, sponge
cards.text_html:
Materials that can be compressed or scratched easily are called soft materials
For example: cotton, sponge

cards.question: Some materials that are difficult to compress are called hard. For example: iron, and most of other metals.
cards.text_html:
Some materials that are difficult to compress are called hard.
For example: iron, and most of other metals.

cards.question: Solubility of liquid in water: Some liquids get completely mixed with water, they are called miscible in water.
cards.text_html: Solubility of liquid in water: Some liquids get completely mixed with water, they are called miscible in water.

cards.question: Solubility of liquid in water: Some liquids do not mix with water and form a separate layer, they are called immiscible in water.
cards.text_html: Solubility of liquid in water: Some liquids do not mix with water and form a separate layer, they are called immiscible in water.

cards.question: Solubility of gases in water : Some gases are soluble in water whereas some are not. Plants and animals that live in water use the oxygen dissolved in water for their survival. Nitrogen, hydrogen, and marsh gas do not dissolve in water so they are insoluble in water.
cards.text_html:
Solubility of gases in water: Some gases are soluble in water whereas some are not.
Plants and animals that live in water use the oxygen dissolved in water for their survival.
Nitrogen, hydrogen, and marsh gas do not dissolve in water so they are insoluble in water.

cards.question: Float or sink: Materials that are insoluble in water either float on the surface or sink in water. Light materials float in water. There are many things floating in the water balloon, dry leaf, ball, dry wood, oil, etc, Heavy materials sink in water. E.g. metals, stones, etc. The keys, stones, rods, bricks, glass and etc sink in water.
cards.text_html:
Float or sink: Materials that are insoluble in water either float on the surface or sink in water. Light materials float in water. There are many things floating in the water balloon, dry leaf, ball, dry wood, oil, etc,
Heavy materials sink in water. E.g. metals, stones, etc. The keys, stones, rods, bricks, glass and etc sink in water.

cards.question: Transparent: The material which allows light to pass through it is called transparent, e.g. acrylic sheet, glass, water, air, etc.
cards.text_html: Transparent: The material which allows light to pass through it is called transparent, e.g. acrylic sheet, glass, water, air, etc.

cards.question: Opaque: The material which does not allow light to pass through it is called opaque, e.g. wood, iron, asbestos, etc.
cards.text_html: Opaque: The material which does not allow light to pass through it is called opaque, e.g. wood, iron, asbestos, etc.

cards.question: Translucent: The material through which light can pass partially is called translucent, e.g. butter paper, thin curtain, etc.
cards.text_html: Translucent: The material through which light can pass partially is called translucent, e.g. butter paper, thin curtain, etc.

cards.question: Materials can be classified on the basis of physical state; such as solid, liquid, and gas. Solid: Solid has a definite shape and definite volume. Examples: Stones, wood, plastic, common salt, steel, ice, glass, etc. Liquid: Liquid has an indefinite shape but a definite volume. Examples: Water, milk, oil, etc. Gas: Gas has an indefinite shape and indefinite volume. Examples: Oxygen, nitrogen, carbon dioxide, etc.
cards.text_html:
Materials can be classified on the basis of physical state; such as solid, liquid, and gas.
Solid: Solid has a definite shape and definite volume. Examples: Stones, wood, plastic, common salt, steel, ice, glass, etc.
Liquid: Liquid has an indefinite shape but a definite volume. Examples: Water, milk, oil, etc.
Gas: Gas has an indefinite shape and indefinite volume. Examples: Oxygen, nitrogen, carbon dioxide, etc.

cards.question: Metals: Materials that are hard, have a such luster, and are good conductors of heat and electricity are called metals, e.g. iron, copper, gold, etc.
cards.text_html: Metals: Materials that are hard, have a such luster, and are good conductors of heat and electricity are called metals, e.g. iron, copper, gold, etc.

cards.question: Non-metal: Materials that are brittle, which don’t have a luster, and are bad conductors of heat and electricity are called non-metals, e.g. coal, chalk, rubber, soil, etc.
cards.text_html: Non-metal: Materials that are brittle, which don’t have a luster, and are bad conductors of heat and electricity are called non-metals, e.g. coal, chalk, rubber, soil, etc.
